content.text: On-call is a tax on engineers' time and sleep. When managed well, the tax is predictable, bounded, and compensated. When managed poorly, it produces chronic sleep deprivation, alert fatigue, degraded judgment, and attrition — all of which directly reduce reliability. The engineering team that burns out its on-call rotation will have worse reliability outcomes than the team that spends time managing on-call health.

Fatigue management is not a 'people problem' that HR handles — it is a reliability engineering problem. An exhausted on-call engineer misses alerts, makes errors in incident response, and cannot perform the post-incident analysis that prevents recurrences. The investments in alert tuning, runbook quality, and rotation sizing are investments in on-call sustainability.

On-call fatigue produces the same symptoms as a reliability incident: degraded response time, increased error rate, and reduced capacity. It just appears in the team's health metrics rather than the service's health metrics.

attrs.alt: On-call page volume by week — green (healthy ≤5), yellow (caution 5–10), red (danger >10 pages/week)

content.text: No action required; maintain alert quality
Review alert classes: which alerts are driving volume? Can any be reduced or eliminated?
Investigate immediately; identify top-3 noisy alerts and reduce them within the current rotation period
Escalate to SRE lead; consider temporarily moving the engineer off primary rotation until volume is reduced

After-hours pages — those fired outside the engineer's normal working hours — are weighted more heavily in fatigue calculations than daytime pages. A page at 3 AM disrupts sleep, which has a 24–48 hour recovery cost that a page at 2 PM does not. Track after-hours pages separately from total pages.
Review alert sensitivity in off-hours windows; consider reducing P2 alerting to P1 only off-hours
Engineer will be sleep-deprived; reduce off-hours alerts urgently

An underdocumented fatigue signal is increasing mean time to acknowledge (MTTA). When an engineer's MTTA climbs from a historical 2–3 minutes to 7–10 minutes over several rotations, it is a strong signal that they are silencing pager alerts during sleep — a behavior that develops when alert volume becomes unmanageable.
Track MTTA per engineer per rotation period.
An MTTA increase of > 2x from historical baseline should trigger a conversation, not a disciplinary action.
The cause is almost always alert volume — the engineer is not lazy, they are overwhelmed.

Compensation for on-call duty varies widely across companies and roles. The key principle is that compensation must be commensurate with the actual burden — a week of 0 pages is not the same as a week of 15 pages, and a flat stipend treats them identically. More equitable models tie at least a portion of compensation to actual burden.
Fixed amount per week on-call regardless of page volume
Does not reflect actual burden; no incentive to reduce alert noise
Fixed amount per page received during the on-call week
Directly reflects burden; incentivizes alert reduction
Unpredictable; can create perverse incentives to not investigate root causes
Base stipend + incremental payment above a page threshold
Reflects burden above a baseline; predictable for low-page weeks
Requires tracking; threshold must be calibrated
After-hours incidents earn compensatory time off at a multiplier
Engineers value time off often more than money; directly addresses fatigue
Requires manager tracking; can create team capacity issues if many engineers take comp time simultaneously
Base stipend + comp time for after-hours + escalation bonus for complex incidents
Recommended Approach for Claude Services Teams
For teams with variable alert volumes (which is most teams during and after a launch), a tiered model with comp time for after-hours incidents is the most equitable:
Base weekly stipend: compensates for the availability constraint and any daytime incidents (up to 5 pages).
Incremental payment: a per-page rate for each page above 5 per week, reflecting actual additional burden.
Comp time: for any after-hours incident requiring more than 30 minutes of work, 1.5 hours of comp time earned.
Post-incident comp time: for any P0 incident requiring more than 2 hours of active work, comp time granted at manager discretion.

The compensation model should make the answer to 'should I reduce alert volume?' obviously yes, for both engineers and managers. If there is no financial difference between 3 pages per week and 20 pages per week, there is no financial incentive to reduce noise.
Compensation addresses the retrospective cost of on-call burden. Fatigue mitigation addresses the prospective cost — reducing the burden before it accumulates.

Alert tuning is the highest-leverage fatigue mitigation strategy. A single noisy alert class that fires 3–4 times per week can double total alert volume. The on-call engineer should have a standing mandate to suppress or reduce any alert that fires more than once per week without requiring action — and to document why the suppression was correct, so it can be reviewed.
Threshold too sensitive; transient spike fires alert
Raise threshold, or require sustained breach for N minutes before alerting
Alert fires every night at the same time
Known batch job or maintenance window
Silence during known window; document the silence
Alert fires, investigation shows no user impact
Alert not measuring the right thing
Revisit SLI — is this metric actually correlated with user experience?
Same alert fires multiple times per incident
Add an inhibit rule in AlertManager to suppress re-fires while the first alert is active
Alert fires for a non-actionable condition
Either add a runbook and action, or convert to a metric on the dashboard (not an alert)

Bringing new rotation members on as shadows — observing incidents without primary responsibility — reduces the fear of on-call for newcomers and distributes knowledge before the engineer takes their first live shift. A shadow week with a senior on-call provides much more value than any amount of documentation.
Shadow week: new engineer observes all incidents, attempts triage before the primary responds, and debrefs after each incident.
Shadow is expected to identify runbook gaps — fresh eyes find steps that experienced engineers assume are obvious.
Primary acts as secondary during shadow week: confirms or corrects the shadow's triage decisions in real time.

During hypercare and in the first 30 days of a new engineer's first solo rotation, an on-call buddy is available for non-paging support: the buddy can be reached via Slack to discuss whether an alert needs escalation, review a diagnosis tree decision, or confirm that a mitigation step is correct. The buddy is not an escalation target — they are a support mechanism for the primary.

Weekly rotations are the most common and generally the most sustainable. Shorter rotations (4 days) reduce individual burden per shift but increase handoff frequency; daily rotations are only practical for very large teams. Longer rotations (2 weeks) reduce handoff overhead but extend the maximum continuous on-call burden.
Very large teams (10+ SREs); services with high alert volume
Most teams; balances handoff overhead with on-call duration
Small teams with very low alert volume; careful fatigue monitoring required

Engineers should not be on call during situations where their ability to respond would be severely compromised. A clear exemption policy prevents ambiguity and reduces the pressure on engineers to stay on the rotation when they should not be.
Vacations and time off: engineer is off the primary rotation. A named substitute must be assigned before the vacation starts — the rotation does not self-heal.
Medical leave: immediate exemption; substitute assigned by manager.
New parent leave: engineer is exempt from on-call for the duration of parental leave.
Bereavement: immediate exemption for the duration of bereavement leave.
Cross-timezone travel: engineer traveling across more than 6 time zones may request a rotation swap for the week of travel and recovery.

When an engineer takes an exemption, a substitute must be identified and confirmed before the exemption period begins. The substitute should be the next in the rotation — not a random assignment. The outgoing engineer is responsible for notifying the substitute and completing the handoff process.

# oncall_swap.py — request and confirm on-call swap
from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import date
import requests

PD_TOKEN = "your-pagerduty-token"

@dataclass
class OnCallSwapRequest:
    requester: str          # PagerDuty user ID
    substitute: str         # PagerDuty user ID
    start_date: date
    end_date: date
    reason: str             # vacation | medical | parental | other

def request_oncall_swap(
    schedule_id: str,
    swap: OnCallSwapRequest,
) -> dict:
    """Create an on-call override in PagerDuty for the swap period."""
    payload = {
        "override": {
            "type": "override",
            "start": f"{swap.start_date}T18:00:00Z",  # rotation handoff time
            "end": f"{swap.end_date}T18:00:00Z",
            "user": {"type": "user_reference", "id": swap.substitute},
        }
    }
    resp = requests.post(
        f"https://api.pagerduty.com/schedules/{schedule_id}/overrides",
        headers={
            "Authorization": f"Token token={PD_TOKEN}",
            "Content-Type": "application/json",
            "From": swap.requester,
        },
        json=payload,
    )
    resp.raise_for_status()
    return resp.json()["override"]


# Example: Alice requests vacation swap for the week of Apr 28
swap = OnCallSwapRequest(
    requester="PALICE",
    substitute="PBOB",
    start_date=date(2024, 4, 28),
    end_date=date(2024, 5, 5),
    reason="vacation",
)

override = request_oncall_swap(schedule_id="SABC123", swap=swap)
print(f"Override created: {override['id']}")
# Bob is now primary for the week of Apr 28 – May 5.
On-call health cannot be managed without measurement. The following metrics should be tracked and reviewed in the monthly cross-functional review alongside error budget data.
Pages per engineer per on-call week
After-hours pages per engineer per week
Escalation Path When Fatigue Is Identified

When a health report flags DANGER status, there is a defined escalation path. This is not punitive — it is a reliability protection mechanism. A fatigued on-call engineer is a system vulnerability. The path below applies to any engineer who crosses the DANGER threshold (>10 pages/week or >5 after-hours pages/week) for two consecutive weeks.
Week 1 DANGER: manager is automatically notified by health report. Engineer and manager discuss root cause — is this a noisy alert, an incident spike, or rotation undercoverage?
Week 2 DANGER (consecutive): engineer is temporarily removed from primary rotation and placed as shadow. A substitute takes the primary slot.
Root cause resolved: engineer returns to rotation at next cycle start after explicit sign-off from manager.
If root cause is structural (rotation too small, too many noisy alerts), it becomes a P1 engineering task with a 2-week SLA.
Engineer moved to shadow; substitute assigned
Review off-hours alert sensitivity; tune before next shift
Runbook review; shadowing of next 3 incidents

On-call programs evolve through distinct maturity stages. Understanding where your team sits helps prioritize the next improvement investment. The goal is not to skip stages — each stage requires the foundations of the prior one.
No formal rotation; everyone is always on-call; no runbooks; incidents handled ad hoc
Formal rotation with primary/secondary; basic runbooks; PagerDuty or equivalent; weekly pages tracked
Reduce noisy alerts; add compensation policy
Fatigue thresholds enforced; compensation tied to actual burden; monthly health reviews; shadow program for new members
Automate health reporting; tune alert SLOs
Alert tuning driven by health data; MTTA trends drive runbook improvements; on-call burden used as SLO feedback signal
Predictive fatigue detection; per-alert cost attribution

Most teams adding Claude API integration to their service start at Stage 1 (the Claude integration is new and on-call behavior is inherited from the existing rotation) or Stage 2 (basic rotation exists but fatigue and compensation policies are not yet formalized). The Stage 2 → Stage 3 transition is where the policies and tooling in this document become critical.
The most reliable signal that you have reached Stage 3 is that on-call engineers stop skipping the health report review because they trust it will surface real problems — not noise.

On-call compensation and fatigue management are reliability engineering concerns, not HR concerns. An exhausted on-call engineer produces worse incident responses — slower acknowledgment, more errors, less thorough post-incident analysis. Fatigue indicators include page volume above 10/week, after-hours pages above 5/week, and MTTA degradation from baseline. Compensation should reflect actual burden — a tiered stipend plus comp time for after-hours work is more equitable than a flat weekly payment. Alert tuning is the highest-leverage fatigue reduction strategy: a single noisy alert class resolved can recover 30–50% of alert volume. Shadow weeks, on-call buddies, and standard exemption policies reduce the access and knowledge barriers that make on-call stressful for new rotation members. All on-call health metrics should be tracked and reviewed monthly alongside SLO data — a team with good SLOs but a burning-out on-call rotation is not in a stable state.
Fatigue thresholds: ≤5 pages/week healthy; 5–10 caution; >10 danger — investigate immediately.
After-hours pages weighted more heavily: ≤2/week healthy; >5/week danger — adjust off-hours alert sensitivity.
Compensation: tiered stipend (base + per-page above threshold) + comp time for after-hours incidents.
Alert tuning is the highest-leverage mitigation: target the top-3 noisy alert classes each rotation.
Shadow weeks for new rotation members: observe real incidents before taking solo shifts.
Exemption policy: vacations, medical, parental leave, bereavement — named substitute required before exemption starts.
